question: Nursing research is a systematic investigation designed to produce generalizable knowledge applicable beyond a single clinical setting.
Evidence-based practice (EBP) integrates the best available evidence, clinical expertise, and patient preferences to guide nursing care.
The main goal of quality improvement (QI) is to create new theories and test ideas.
Randomized controlled trials generally provide a higher level of evidence than expert opinion.
Quality improvement projects always need Institutional Review Board (IRB) approval before starting.
Quantitative research focuses on numerical data and statistical analysis to uncover patterns, identify relationships, or test interventions.
Evidence-based practice (EBP) is different from research because it uses current evidence instead of creating new knowledge.
A review of the literature is an essential component of nursing research, evidence-based practice (EBP), and quality improvement (QI) initiatives.
Statistical significance alone determines whether research findings are clinically meaningful for nursing practice.
Nurses at every practice level help identify issues for research, evidence-based practice, or quality improvement.
